coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent probes for “off–on” highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Cui, Peipei
Zhang, Feng
Feng, Xiaoting
Wang, Yaling
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized by a facile, and low-cost one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source for the first time. The obtained NCDs show stable blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 35.4%, along with the fluorescence lifetime of ca. 6.75ns. Most importantly, Hg2+ can completely quench the fluorescence of NCDs as a result of the formation of a non-fluorescent stable NCDs–Hg2+ complex. Static fluorescence quenching towards Hg2+ is proved by the Stern–Volmer equation, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra, temperature dependent quenching and fluorescence lifetime measurements. Subsequently, the fluorescence of the NCDs–Hg2+ system is completely recovered with the addition L-cysteine (L-Cys) owing to the dissociation of NCDs–Hg2+ complex to form a more stable Hg2+-L-Cys complex by Hg2+–S bonding. Therefore, such NCDs can be used as an effective fluorescent “turn-off” probe for rapid, rather highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1.48nM and a linear detection range of 0–10μM. Interestingly, NCDs–Hg2+ system can be conveniently employed as a fluorescent “turn-on” sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of L-Cys with a low LOD of 0.79nM and a wide linear detection range of 0–50μM. Further, the sensitivity of NCDs to Hg2+ is preserved in tap water with a LOD of 1.65nM and a linear detection range of 0–10μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Hg2+
L-Cys
High selectivity
High sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and L-Cys
Synthesis and structure characterization of the NCDs
Feasibility of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism
Sensitivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
Selectivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
Feasibility and sensitivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in tap water
Feasibility of NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample
Sensitivity of NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample
Selectivityof NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of NCDs, the inset of (b) is the histogram of NCDs size distributions. (c) XRD patterns of NCDs. (d) FTIR spectra of NCDs.
(a) XPS, (b) C1s, (c) O1s and (d) N1s spectra of NCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of NCDs aqueous solutions. Insets in (a) show optical photographs of NCDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light (365nm). (b) PL spectra of NCDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths. Inset in (b) shows maximum emission wavelength plotted against excitation wavelength.

(a) PL spectra of NCDs aqueous solutions at different concentrations of NaCl (λ ex=360nm). (b) UV–vis absorption and (c) PL spectra of NCDs aqueous solutions at different pH values. Insets in (a) and (c) show the relative fluorescence intensity of NCDs aqueous solutions at 440nm under different concentrations of NaCl solution and different pH values, separately. (d) Concentration dependent behavior of NCDs in aqueous solution.

(a) PL and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs, NCDs–Hg2+ mixture and NCDs-–Hg2+-L-Cys mixture (pH 7.0, 0.025 M PBS, λ ex=360 nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1, [Hg2+]=50μM, [L-Cys]=75μM). Inset of (b) shows amplified UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs, NCDs–Hg2+ mixture and NCDs–Hg2+-L-Cys mixture).

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs by adding different amount of Hg2+ with emission monitored at 440nm and excitations at 375nm. (b) The linear relationship of F 0/F versus the concentrations of Hg2+ ions over the range of 0–10μM at different temperatures. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements (pH 7.0, 0.025M PBS, λ ex=360nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1; F 0 and F are NCDs aqueous solutions fluorescence intensities at 440nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of NCDs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.5, 5, 7, 8.5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60μM) in deionized water. Inset shows the dependence of F/F 0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–60μM. (b) Plots of the values of F 0/F at 440nm versus the concentrations of Hg2+. Inset shows the linear relationship of F 0/F versus the concentrations of Hg2+ ions over the range of 0–10μM in deionized water. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements (pH 7.0, 0.025M PBS, λ ex=360nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1; F 0 and F are NCDs aqueous solutions fluorescence intensities at 440nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of the NCDs aqueous solution in the presence of different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence response of the NCDs aqueous solution in the presence of different metal ions at 440nm (pH 7.0, 0.025M PBS, λ ex=360nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1, [Mn+]=50μM; F 0 and F are NCDs aqueous solutions PL intensities at 440nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of NCDs–Hg2+ system in the presence of increasing concentration of L-Cys (from bottom to top: 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, and 75μM). (b) A linear relationship of F 2/F 1 versus the concentration of L-Cys over the range from 0 to 50μM. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three measurements (pH 7.0, 0.025M PBS, λ ex=360nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1, [Hg2+]=50μM; F 1 and F 2 are the PL intensities of NCDs–Hg2+ system at 440nm in the absence and presence of L-Cys, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of the NCDs–Hg2+ system in presence of L-GSH, DL-Hcy or L-Cys with a concentration of 55μM. (b) Fluorescence response of the NCDs–Hg2+ system in the presence of L-GSH, DL-Hcy or L-Cys at 440nm. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements (pH 7.0, 0.025M PBS, λ ex=360nm, [NCDs]=16μgmL−1, [Hg2+]=50μM; F 1 and F 2 are NCDs–Hg2+ system PL intensities at 440 nm in the absence and presence of L-GSH, DL-Hcy or L-Cys, respectively).
Schematic of the preparation for NCDs and their application for Hg2+ and L-Cys detection.
Comparison of LOD and linear range for L-Cys using different CDs–Hg2+ fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent probes for “off–on” highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized by a facile, and low-cost one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source for the first time. The obtained NCDs show stable blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 35.4%, along with the fluorescence lifetime of ca. 6.75ns. Most importantly, Hg2+ can completely quench the fluorescence of NCDs as a result of the formation of a non-fluorescent stable NCDs–Hg2+ complex. Static fluorescence quenching towards Hg2+ is proved by the Stern–Volmer equation, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra, temperature dependent quenching and fluorescence lifetime measurements. Subsequently, the fluorescence of the NCDs–Hg2+ system is completely recovered with the addition L-cysteine (L-Cys) owing to the dissociation of NCDs–Hg2+ complex to form a more stable Hg2+-L-Cys complex by Hg2+–S bonding. Therefore, such NCDs can be used as an effective fluorescent “turn-off” probe for rapid, rather highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1.48nM and a linear detection range of 0–10μM. Interestingly, NCDs–Hg2+ system can be conveniently employed as a fluorescent “turn-on” sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of L-Cys with a low LOD of 0.79nM and a wide linear detection range of 0–50μM. Further, the sensitivity of NCDs to Hg2+ is preserved in tap water with a LOD of 1.65nM and a linear detection range of 0–10μM.
Fluorescence turn “off-on” probes based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be used for Hg2+ and L-cysteine detection in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (II) (Hg2+), the most stable inorganic form of mercury with difficult biodegradability, is among the most hazardous and ubiquitous environmental contaminants with the feature of bioaccumulation and high toxic properties and has raised serious environmental and health problems [1,2]. As a biological thiol, L-Cysteine (L-Cys) plays crucial roles in maintaining physiological redox homeostasis through the equilibrium established between reduced free thiols and oxidized disulfides in vivo [3–5]. Abnormal levels of L-Cys in biological systems are associated with many human diseases, such as slow growth, hair depigmentation, Alzheimer's disease and cardiovascular disease [3,5–7]. Therefore, the relatively inexpensive, rapid, sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and L-Cys in biological and environmental samples is very important and has attracted a great deal of attention [6]. Fluorescence assay has its advantages of being non-sample-destructive, ultrasensitive and simple [3,5]. So far, various fluorescent probes are currently being actively developed, such as organic dye molecules [5,8], metal nanoparticles [9] and colloid semiconductor quantum dots [9]. However, the above fluorescent materials are limited by the fact that they are toxic, low sensitive, low selective, hydrophobic or costly [8,10]. Thus, a simple, inexpensive, environment-friendly and stable probe for not only detecting but also quantifying Hg2+ and L-Cys in environmental samples with a lower detection limit is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) with sensing capability have attracted significant research attention. In view of the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs in the detection process, they have been used as fluorescent probes for sensing some metal cations (Hg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Sn2+) and biomolecules [L-Cys, homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH)] [11–15]. CDs potentially offer advantages such as high sensitivity, economical and convenient detection procedures [12]. However, there are still some drawbacks, including relatively low fluorescence quantum yield (QY, usually less than 10%), low selectivity, high detection limits and lack of evidence for a reliable mechanism for fluorescence quenching or enhancing [4,16]. Compared with CDs, nitrogen doped CDs (NCDs) usually exhibit larger QY (usually more than 10%), while retaining all the advantages of blank CDs [17]. Moreover, improved QY of NCDs contributes to improving the detection sensitivity and selevtivity of CDs towards Hg2+ and L-Cys. In our previous work [2], we have demonstrated that the introduction of an amine-group enriched organic molecule (urea) can increase QY to 42.2%. However, as-prepared NCDs can only be used as an effective fluorescent “turn–off” sensor for highly selective detection of Hg2+, with relatively high LOD (2.91nM) in a narrow linear range (0–50nM), and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Hg2+ towards NCDs was not investigated in detail. Therefore, the aims of this work are (1) to improve the LOD and linear range of NCDs towards Hg2+ by improving the QY of NCDs; (2) to construct a fluorescent “off-on” sensor for Hg2+ and L-Cys, respectively, in the same NCD sample; (3) to investigate the detection mechanism during the two fluorescent detection processes involving Hg2+ and L-Cys.

In this study, we report for the first time a hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of NCDs by using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source. The obtained NCDs show blue fluorescence with an increased QY of 35.4%. Most importantly, we found as-prepared NCDs can serve as an effective fluorescent “turn-off” probe by forming a non-fluorescent stable NCDs–Hg2+ complex for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a low limit of detection (LOD, 1.48nM) and a wider linear detection range of 0–10μM. Interestingly, NCDs–Hg2+ system can also be conveniently employed as a fluorescent “turn-on” sensor by dissociation of NCDs–Hg2+ complexes to form a more stable Hg2+-L-Cys complex through Hg2+–S bonding for highly sensitive and selective detection of L-Cys with a low LOD of 0.79nM and a wider linear detection range of 0–50μM. ( Scheme 1).

Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 25wt%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37wt%), nitric acid (HNO3, 65wt%), phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.025M, pH 7.0) and L-Cys were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company. Quinine sulfate (suitable for fluorescence) was supplied by Sinopharm. NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, CdCl2·21/2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, AlCl3, Pb(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, SnCl2·2H2O and Hg(NO3)2·H2O were obtained from Jiangyan Huanqiu Reagent factory. All metal ion stock solutions were prepared from nitrate salts or chloride salts. CH3COONa, NaNO3, NaClO4, (CH3COO)2Hg, HgCl2 and Hg(ClO4)2 were purchased from Aladdin. L-GSH was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. DL-Hcy was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Certified reference mercury solution (CRMS) was purchased from China Standard Material Procurement Center. Dialysis bag (1000Da, molecular weight cutoff) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. All reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Real sample tap water was obtained from our laboratotry.

Photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the NCDs were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer with xenon lamp as the source of excitation. Fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs were recorded with an Edinburgh FLS980 time-corrected single photon counting (TCSPC) system under right-angle sample geometry using laser. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were collected on a Hitachi U3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer using a 1 cm path length quartz cell at room temperature. The morphology and microstructure of NCDs were examined by a JEOL JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operating at 200kV. The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of NCDs solution on carbon-coated copper grid followed by drying in air. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data for the NCD solutions deposited on glass substrates were measured by a Kratos AXIS Ultra spectrometer with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6eV) for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Binding energies were calibrated with C1s (284.8eV). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a BRUKER TENSOR27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer with the potassium bromide pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku-D/MAX 2500 diffractometer equipped with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα (λ=0.15405nm) radiation at a scan speed of 4°•min−1 in 10–90° (2θ ranges). Elemental analysis (C, H, N) was performed using a Perkin Elmer 2400 analyzer.

The NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (as the carbon source) and ammonia solution (as the nitrogen source), as shown in Scheme 1. In a typical synthesis procedure, 4.2g of citric acid was dissolved in 40mL of deionized water and then mixed with 0.06mol (4.5mL) of ammonia solution under vigorous stirring to form a transparent solution. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 8h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. Then, the obtained black–green dispersion was filtered with a 0.22μm filter membrane and then dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff 1000Da for 24h (changing deionized water every 8h) to remove unreacted raw materials and low molecular weight by-products. Finally, the obtained pure NCDs solution was frozen in −86°C freezer for 20min and then was lyophilized for 24h in a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain 1.582g of black–green solid powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Hg2+ was performed at room temperature in PBS. To evaluate the sensitivity towards Hg2+, in a typical assay, purified NCDs dispersion (100μL, 8mgmL−1), about 40mL of PBS and different volumes of Hg2+ stock solution (0.005M) were successively added into 50mL volumetric flask, and finally diluted with PBS to 50mL. After thorough mixing, the PL spectra were recorded immediately (equilibrated time only 30s). The selectivity for Hg2+ was confirmed by adding 500μL of single metal ion stock solution (0.005M) instead of Hg2+ in a similar way.

Then, for detection of L-Cys, purified NCDs dispersion (100μL, 8mgmL−1), about 40mL of PBS and 500μL of Hg2+ stock solution (0.005M) were successively added into 50mL volumetric flask. After thorough shaking and equilibration for 30s, different volumes of L-Cys stock solution (0.05M) were added to above mixture solution, and finally the solution was diluted with PBS to 50mL. After thorough mixing, the PL spectra were recorded immediately (equilibrated time only 5s). The final concentration of Hg2+ was 50μM.

All the fluorescence detections were under the same conditions: the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 2nm, and the PL spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 360nm with the emission recorded over the wavelength range of 370–700nm. The fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission peak was used for quantitative and qualitative analysis. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure characterization of the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method, as illustrated in Scheme 1. TEM techniques were used to obtain information about the size and shape of the NCDs. The TEM and HRTEM images of NCDs ( Fig. 1a and b) reveal that the as-prepared NCDs are well dispersed without apparent aggregation. The NCDs are mainly distributed in the range of 1–3nm with an average size of 2nm and possess a nearly spherical shape (Fig. 1b). No obvious lattice fringes are found in the HRTEM image of the NCDs (Fig. 1b), which is indicative of their amorphous nature, as also reported by other researchers [18,19]. The typical XRD patterns of the NCDs (Fig. 1c) display only one broader diffraction peak centered at 2θ=29°, which is also assigned to highly disordered carbon structure [20]. The XRD results, along with HRTEM data, illustrate that NCDs are amorphous nanoparticles without discernible lattice structures.

The functional groups on the surface of NCDs were investigated using FTIR spectrometry (Fig. 1d). Bands at 3425 and 3198cm−1 are characteristic absorption of O–H and N–H stretching vibration, respectively [21–24]. Bands at 1691 and 1580cm−1 are respectively assigned to stretching vibration of C=O [21–24] and C–N [25]. Bands at 2928 and 2857cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching vibration [5,21–23,26]. Bands at 1320 and 1400cm−1 are ascribed to the typical stretching vibration modes of C=N and C–N heterocycles [10]. The results demonstrate that the as-synthesized NCDs are functionalized by carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups, which would impart excellent solubility in water without the need of further chemical modification of nanoparticle surface and thus greatly widen their further sensing application in aqueous system. The surface chemical and elemental compositions of NCDs were further characterized by XPS technique on the basis of the software of XPS PEAK4.1. As shown in Fig. 2a, three dominant peaks at 283, 398 and 529eV (ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively) demonstrate the existence of C, N and O elements in the prepared NCDs [21,27]. In particular, Fig. 2b presents the typical C1s spectrum, exhibiting three distinct peaks located at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.4eV, which are ascribed to graphitic or aliphatic C–C/C=C [27,28], C–N/C–O [29,30], C=O/C=N [31,32], respectively. The spectrum of O1s is displayed in Fig. 2c, in which C=O and C–OH/C–O–C groups produce two peaks at 531.4 and 532.6eV [5,9,27], respectively. For the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), two peaks at 399.9 and 401.3eV are observed due to pyrrolic-like N and N–H groups, respectively [10,21,22,27,30]. The XPS results are in agreement with the FTIR measurements of NCDs. The results of elemental analysis demonstrate that the composition of as-prepared NCDs is 39.92wt% C, 15.14wt% N, 6.51wt% H, and 38.43wt% O (calculated). XPS spectra and FTIR spectra, along with elemental analysis, confirm that the NCDs are nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich nanodots functionalized with hydroxyl, amide, amino, carboxyl/carbonyl moieties.

As for the optical properties of the NCDs, Fig. 3a shows typical UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the NCDs aqueous solution. It can be seen that there are two absorbance peaks located at around 235 and 335nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peak at 235nm can be ascribed to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic C=C and C–C in sp2 hybrid domains, which can not produce observable fluorescence signal [9,33,34]. The other absorption peak at 335nm, which corresponds to the n→π* transition of C=O or C–OH bond in sp3 hybrid regions, originates from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can lead to strong fluorescence [9,33,34]. Accordingly, as shown in Fig. 3a, there are two optimal excitation peaks around 250 and 360nm. When excited at 360nm, the NCDs exhibit maximum PL emission centered at 440nm. The QY of the as-prepared NCDs reaches as high as 35.4% when excited at 360nm and compared with quinine sulfate as a standard (Fig. S1†). In the photographs shown in Fig. 3a inset, the diluted NCDs aqueous solution is light green (left inset of Fig. 3a) under ambient daylight but exhibits a very intense blue color (right inset of Fig. 3a), which corresponds to their PL spectra (Fig. 3a and b) and high QY when excited with a UV light (365nm), further indicating the strong fluorescent property of the NCDs. The strong fluorescence of the NCDs may result from the emissive traps of the nitrogen-doped surface [34]. In addition, the sample solution is highly transparent under ambient conditions, suggesting the excellent aqueous dispersibility of the NCDs owing to a large number of surface-attached hydrophilic amino groups (inset of Fig. 3a) [31].

Fig. 3b shows the PL emission spectra of the NCDs aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths. The evolution of NCDs emission with excitation wavelength can be separated into two regions. Above 380nm, the prepared NCDs feature excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior, the maximum emission peak shows red-shift from 440 to 520nm and the peak intensity decreases with increasing excitation wavelength from 380 to 500nm. In contrast, the emission peak position shows no evident shift and remains at 440nm and peak intensity first increases and then gradually decreases and reaches a maximum at excitation wavelength of 360 over 300–380nm (Fig. 3b). Such excitation-dependent PL behavior is related to the different surface states of the NCDs. There are multiple C–, N– and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the produced NCDs, resulting in various surface states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Under different excitation wavelength, the corresponding surface state emissive trap is dominant, giving excitation-dependent emission [15,34]. However, the observed excitation-independent emission over 300–380nm indicates relatively uniform surface state emissive traps, which can avoid autofluorescence during their applications [10].

The reaction conditions were optimized such as reaction temperature, reaction time and the volume of ammonia solution for preparation of high-luminescence NCDs. In dilute solution, the absorbance of NCDs at 335nm and the PL intensity at 440nm are used to estimate the concentration of NCDs. The concentration of NCDs is proportional to the absorbance at 335nm and the PL intensity at 440nm [2]. In subsequent experiments, the mass of citric acid (4.2g) and the volume of water (40mL) were fixed. The investigation on different reaction temperature (120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220°C) showed that absorption and PL intensity at 440nm increase in 120–180°C range and then decrease in 180–220°C range. NCDs prepard at 180°C have the highest absorbance and the strongest fluorescence (Fig. S2†). Thus 180°C was chosen as the optimum reaction temperature. Then, the effect of different reaction time (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 24h) was investigated at 180°C, showing that absorption and PL intensity at 440nm increase in 1–8h range and then decrease in 8–24h range (Fig. S3†). So the reaction time of 8h was chosen as the optimum reaction time for the next experiments. At last different volume of ammonia solution (0, 0.75, 1.5, 3, 4.5, 6, 9, 18mL) was examined. The absorption and PL intensity at 440nm increase in 0–4.5mL range and then decrease in 4.5–18mL range (Fig. S4†). Therefore, 4.5mL of ammonia solution was selected as the optimum volume of ammonia solution. Thus, the optimum synthesis conditions of NCDs were obtained: reaction temperature 180°C, reaction time 8h and the volume of ammonia solution 4.5mL in 40mL of H2O (molar ratios of amine/carboxyl groups 1:1). The optimized NCDs were used for further characterization and application in Hg2+ and L-Cys detection.

The influence of ionic strength, pH and solution concentration on the optical properties of prepared NCDs was investigated. Although all samples demonstrate only a slight reduction in PL intensity, peak position and peak shape keep constant as the ionic strength of aqueous NaCl medium increases even when the concentration of NaCl is as high as 2.0M ( Fig. 4a), suggesting the excellent optical stability of the NCDs. This finding suggests that NCDs have great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions [19,27].

The pH-dependent behavior of the NCDs was also investigated (Fig. 4b and c). From the UV–vis spectra, the absorption peak at 335nm is blue shifed in a strongly acidic environment (pH=1–5), while maintaining its peak position at 335nm in neutral and basic solution (pH=6–14) (Fig. 4b). From the fluorescence spectra, the PL intensity at 440nm gradually increases with the increase of pH value from 1 to 5 and then shows a steep increase at pH 5–6 and reaches a maximum at pH 7. Finally, the PL intensity of NCDs slightly decreases at pH values larger than 7 (the change less than 10%). Moreover, the PL emission peak is red-shifted in acidic solution (Fig. 4c). It is also found that the pH-response of the PL behavior of NCDs is reversible. The PL intensity at 440nm increases when pH is tuned from 1 to 7 and then slightly decreases at pH values larger than 7 by adding NaOH (w=10%), and afterwards, it returns back to the initial intensity value as pH is changed back from 14 to 1 by dropping HCl (w=10%). Our experiments have also showed that the pH-response is highly repetitive, i.e., after 10 cycles of changing pH from 1 to 14 and then from 14 to 1 in the same solution, the PL intensity at 440nm remains virtually unchanged. These results may imply that the surface state of the NCDs is affected by the surrounding pH. The variation in UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence intensity of NCDs may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl group and amino group on the NCDs surface [2,35], which affect the PL center of blue emissions [36]. This phenomenon further confirms that the carboxyl group and amino group are present on the surface of NCDs. In our study, the NCDs were also investigated to detect metal ions and L-Cys, and therefore, we chose pH value of 7 as the optimum pH value.

Self-absorption quenching is often observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials. The concentration-dependent PL behavior was measured to investigate collisional quenching in the NCDs [37]. The concentration-dependent PL is shown in Fig. 4d. The PL intensity at 440nm first gradually increases in 0–0.096mgmL−1 range and then gradually decreases in 0.096–1.28mgmL−1 range and finally reaches a platform when concentration of NCDs is 1.28mgmL−1. When the concentration is high enough, collisional quenching occurs and the PL intensity decreases. The concentration of NCDs used should be carefully selected for practical applications (0.016mgmL−1 is used in the following experiments).

Furthermore, it is found that the NCDs still exhibit good photostability when stored at room temperature for about six months. When NCDs solid powder is redispersed in water, the solution exhibits strong PL with no aggregation. Solid NCDs are very convenient for storage, transportation and quantitative use. These results reveal that the NCDs have good photostability, which is of great potential in ion detection application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The high QY renders NCDs a possible type of promising fluorescence probe for sensing metal ions. To research the feasibility of fluorescence, the NCDs fluorescence probe detection of Hg2+ was performed. Scheme 1 shows schematic illustration of the Hg2+ and L-Cys detection mechanism using NCDs. In all ion detection experiments, concentration of NCDs was fixed as 16μgmL−1. Fig. 5a and b show PL and UV–vis absorbance spectra of NCDs and NCDs–Hg2+ mixture (Hg2+ added into NCDs aqueous solution). In Fig. 5a, NCDs aqueous solution exhibits a strong PL peak at 440nm. While the presence of 50μM Hg2+ leads to a substantial decrease of fluorescence intensity at 440nm (quenching efficiency reaches 99.2%), elucidating that Hg2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of NCDs. This observation may be attributed to the fact that Hg2+ has strong binding affinity and fast chelating kinetic with the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino group of NCDs, which changes the electronic structure of NCDs and affects the distribution of excitons, thus facilitating the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, i.e. the excited-state electrons transfer to the LUMO of the Hg2+ cation from the NCDs, and then the electrons return to the ground state of the NCDs in a radiationless transfer, and leading to a significant quenching of the fluorescence of NCDs [2,4,21,38]. Meanwhile, the Hg2+-induced conversion of the functional group (–CONH–) from spirolactam structure to an opened-ring amide may also make important contribution to the fluorescence quenching [34]. In the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5b), upon addition of 50μM Hg2+, the UV–vis absorption of NCDs changes significantly. The absorption centered at 235nm disappears, the absorption peak of NCDs at 335nm (0μM Hg2+) is blue-shifted to 324nm (50μM Hg2+) and a new peak below 220nm appears. We speculate the peak below 220nm comes from the addition of Hg2+. In order to further confirm our assumption, we carried out contrast experiment by gradual addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ (from bottom to top: 0–60μM) in the presence and absence of NCDs to study the viration of absorbance spectra (Fig. S5†). It is clear that, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the UV–vis peak below 220nm becomes much stronger for both cases, indicating the peak below 220nm comes from the absorption peak of Hg2+. In addition, the transition at 235nm due to a π–π* transition in the UV–vis spectra disappears completely upon addition of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5b and S5a†), indicating the loss of conjugated aromatic π systems [39]. The blue-shift of UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs after adding 50μM Hg2+ shows that Hg2+ can change the electronic structure of NCDs by influencing the surface states of NCDs. Thus, Hg2+-induced blue-shift and disappearance of the π–π* transition in the UV–vis spectra suggest that Hg2+ ions bind with the NCDs framework via covalent bonding between an empty orbital of Hg2+ and the π electrons of the NCDs, leading to the change in the electronic distribution of the NCDs framework and the lose in the structural integrity of the NCDs [39]. Above UV–vis results indicate the formation of a stable non-fluorescent NCDs–Hg2+ complex between NCDs and Hg2+, which results in static quenching [39]. With these results, it is obvious the NCDs can rationally detect Hg2+ in aqueous solution through a fluorescence approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To get insight into the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of NCDs by Hg2+, TCSPC experiments were applied to study the exciton behavior of NCDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+. Fluorescence lifetime measurement is the most definitive method to distinguish static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching removes a fraction of the fluorophores. The complexed fluorophores are nonfluorescent, and thus the only observed fluorescence is from the uncomplexed fluorophores. The uncomplexed fraction is unperturbed, and hence the lifetime is τ 0. Therefore, for static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime does not change (τ 0/τ=1), where τ 0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively. In contrast, for dynamic quenching, F 0/F=τ 0/τ, and the lifetime decreases on addition of the quencher [21,40,41]. Fig. 6a presents fluorescence decay curves of NCDs upon addition of different concentration of Hg2+ by monitoring emission at 440nm with the excitation wavelength of 375nm. Results show that the decay curves are close to each other. The fluorescence decay curves could be best fitted with a biexponential function (1) [39,40]:
(1)
R ( t ) =
B
1
exp ( − t /
τ
1
) +
B
2
exp ( − t /
τ
2
)

where R(t) is the intensity usually assumed to decay as the sum of individual single exponential decays, B 1 and B 2 are the pre-exponential factors, τ 1 and τ 2 are the decay times. The average life time τ ave of NCDs upon addition of different concentration of Hg2+ was determined by Eq. (2) [39,40]:
(2)
τ
a v e
=
B
1
τ
1
2
+
B
2
τ
2
2
B
1
τ
1
+
B
2
τ
2

The specific lifetime best-fit results are listed in Table S1†. As shown in Table S1†, the average fluorescence lifetime (τ ave) of pure NCDs is calculated to be 6.750ns, consisting of a short lifetime component of τ 1=1.38ns (2.78%) and a long lifetime component of τ 2=6.87 ns (97.22%). With gradual addition of Hg2+ from 0 to 60μM, the average lifetime of NCDs only decays to 6.443ns, consisting of a short lifetime component of τ 1=0.84ns (5.00%) and a long lifetime component of τ 2=6.79ns (95.00%). The slightly reduced fluorescence lifetime indicates a static quenching occurs owing to the formation of a stable nonfluorescent ground state complex between NCDs and Hg2+. Static quenching does not decrease the lifetime because only the fluorescent molecules are observed, and the uncomplexed fluorophores have the unquenched lifetime τ 0 [40].

In order to obtain further insight into the mechanism of quenching, we investigated the absorption behavior and temperature-dependent behavior. From Fig. 5b, upon addition of 50μM Hg2+, the absorption centered at 235nm disappears, the absorption peak of NCDs at 335nm (0μM Hg2+) is blue-shifted to 324nm (50μM Hg2+) and a new peak below 220nm appears, proving an obvious static intermediate (stable metal complex) between Hg2+ and the NCDs. The static quenching is also proved by the temperature-dependent behavior. K sv values at three temperatures were obtained (Fig. 6b). The calculated values of Ksv are 0.6140 (R 2=0.99946), 0.50725 (R 2=0.99969) and 0.3565 (R 2=0.99972) for 10, 25 and 50°C, respectively. The decrease in K sv values with increasing temperature suggests that the quenching is a static process. Because higher temperature will typically result in the dissociation of weakly bound complexes between Hg2+ and the NCDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: For a sensitivity study, fluorescence spectroscopy measurement was conducted to further evaluate the response of such NCDs towards different Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 0–60μM. As found in Fig. 7a, concentration-dependent quenching is observed: the fluorescence intensity of NCDs at 440nm upon the excitation at 360nm gradually decreases from 0 to 50μM, and then reaches a saturation point as the Hg2+ concentration reaches 50μM, further addition Hg2+concentration (from 50 to 60μM) does not induce detectable changes in the PL spectra, revealing that the system was sensitive to Hg2+ concentration. Moreover, no spectral shift of the emission band is observed. The fluorescence is nearly completely quenched in the presence of 50μM Hg2+ (with a quenching efficiency of 99.2%), so we chose 50μM Hg2+ for the subsequent studies.

Fluorescence quenching refers to any process that decreases the fluorescence intensity of a sample. The quenching effect is well analyzed using the standard Stern–Volmer Eq. (3), for a static or dynamic mechanism [40,42]:
(3)
F
0
/ F = K
sv
[ Q ] + 1

where K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of quencher Hg2+, and F 0, F are the PL intensities of NCDs at 440nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. Quenching data are usually presented as plots of F 0/F versus [Q]. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 7b does not fit a linear calibration plots over the whole Hg2+ concentration range of 0–60μM. In contrast, inset of Fig. 7b reveals that a linear calibration plot for the quantitative analysis of Hg2+ can be fitted between the F 0/F and the concentration of Hg2+ at the range from 0 to 10µM. The slope of the calibration curve, which represents the Stern–Volmer constant, is 0.5068[μM]−1 or 5.068×105 M−1 and the correlation coefficients (R 2) is 0.99979. This order of magnitude of K sv is compatible with the formation of a quite stable complexes (static quenching) between the amine group on the surface of the NCDs and Hg2+ ions [43,44]. At higher concentrations of Hg2+ ( [Hg2+] ≥10μM), there is a deviation from linearity (Fig. 7b), probably due to the presence of both static and dynamic mechanisms as observed by other researchers [15,38,45], which provides further support to our prediction on complex formation in the ground state. The error bars represent variations among three separate measurements. The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was calculated to be 1.48nM using Eq. (4) [12,46]:
(4)
LOD = 3
σ
/
s
,

where σ, which represents the repeatability of the analytical response, is the relative standard deviation of the blank NCDs sample recorded (N=10) and s is the slope of the linear calibration plot. σ=0.025% demonstrats the excellent reliability of the fluorescence chemosensor. Apparently, the obtained LOD of 1.48nM (0.30ppb) is remarkable and satisfies the sensitivity requirement of Hg2+ detection for drinking water of 10nM (=2ppb) permitted by US Environmental Protection Agency, which shows our NCDs are highly sensitive to Hg2+ and can be used in the detection of Hg2+ in deionized water. Table S2† shows the comparison of LOD and linear range with those of different CDs probes for Hg2+ detection in the literature [1,2,4,21,23,24,27,34,45]. Our LOD is lower than most previously reported values. Although our LOD is higher than 0.439 and 0.8nM for the CDs with citric acid [4] and sodium citrate [1] as carbon source, respectively, the linear range is wider. Moreover, our LOD is lower and the linear range is wider than our previously NCDs prepared by citric acid and urea [2]. It is suggested that such NCDs as a fluorescent probe would exhibit superior sensitivity for Hg2+, wide linear response range and high possibility for use in the quantitative detection of Hg2+ in deionized water.

The required time for reaction equilibrium between NCDs and Hg2+ was also investigated. Fig. S6† shows the time-dependent PL spectra of NCDs mixed with 50μM Hg2+. It can be seen that PL intensity at 440nm decreases sharply only within 30s upon the addition of Hg2+ as a result of the quenching of Hg2+ and then shows no change. The result indicates that 30s is required to reach reaction equilibrium between the NCDs and Hg2+. Therefore, PL emission spectrum was collected 30s after mixing NCDs and different concentrations of Hg2+. Such results indicate the quenching kinetics is fairly fast. This rapid response is highly desirable for ion detection.

In order to verify our detection method of Hg2+ is reliable, certified reference mercury solution (CRMS; 1000μg/mL in 1.0 M HNO3) was used (Fig. S7a and b). Three aliquots of 5μM CRMS which were prepared by diluting original CRMS were added into NCDs solution, separately. PL spactra were measured after 30s (Fig. S7c). Then F 0/F is calculated to be 3.578, 3.521 and 3.559, separately. F 0 and F are NCDs aqueous solutions PL intensities at 440nm in the absence and presence of 5μM CRMS, respectively. According to standard the Stern–Volmer equation (F 0/F=0.5068[Hg2+]+1) (inset of Fig. 7b), [Hg2+] is calculated to be 5.087, 4.975 and 5.049, respectively. The average [Hg2+] is calculated to be 5.037μM. Compared with the CRMS’ true value of 5μM, the relative standard deviation is only 0.74%, which indicates that our detection method of Hg2+ has good reliability and repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the selectivity and specificity of NCDs towards Hg2+, three PL screening experiments were carried out. The possible interfering metal ions were allowed to get added into the NCDs aqueous solution. First, the PL intensity changes can be found before and after the addition of other 18 kinds of biologically and environmentally relevant metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Sn2+ and Ag+) alone at a concentration of 50μM under the same conditions as in the case of Hg2+ upon the excitation at 360nm. In Fig. 8, only Hg2+ significantly decreases the fluorescence intensity at 440nm of NCDs (quenching 99.2% of its original values upon excitation wavelength of 360nm), while other metal ions only change the signal so slightly that the influence is in fact negligible under the same experiment conditions. The slight fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the nonspecific interactions between the carboxyl groups and the metal ions, which has little significance for ion detection under practical conditions [19]. Such results directly indicate that NCDs have high selectivity for Hg2+ detection against other metal ions in an aqueous solution.

Competitive experiments were also carried out to identify the interference of other common metal ions (Fig. S8†), in which 50μM single metal ion alone (black bars, Fig. S8†) or the mixtures of 50μM Hg2+ and 50μM single metal ion (red bars, Fig. S8†) were added into the NCDs solution. Then the quenching effects of [M]n+ and the mixtures of Hg2++[M]n+ on the fluorescence of the NCDs were examined. As shown in Fig. S8†, in comparison with the efficient quenching effect of Hg2+, the influence of other single coexisting metal ions is negligible. This phenomenon elucidates that NCDs are insensitive to other metal ions but exclusively sensitive to Hg2+ in the mixtures.

Furthermore, the selectivity of NCDs in coexistence of all possible interference ions was evaluated for considering the cross reactivity, as shown in Fig. S9†. The fluorescence quenching of the probe solution by the addition of Hg2+ is not influenced by the coexistence of other metal ions tested, in other words, the present method can still detect Hg2+ in the presence of all possible interference ions (the concentration of each metal ion in the mixture is 50μM), underlining the high selectivity of NCDs for detecting Hg2+ and the great potential of NCDs for real sample application. The above results indicate that this NCDs-based fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ exhibits high selectivity and specificity for qualitative detection of Hg2+ in aquatic system.

At last, to confirm that the quenching is due to Hg2+ and not due to the anion associated with it, various Hg2+ salts containing different types of anions (chloride, nitrate, acetate and perchlorate salts of Hg2+) were used. All Hg2+ salts display similar quenching effect, proving that Hg2+ is solely responsible for quenching (Fig. S10a†). To prove that the quenching is not due to a combination of Hg2+ and its counter anion, the similar experiments were carried out using various Na+ salts (similar anions). Interestingly, none of the Na+ salts shows quenching of fluorescence of NCDs, indicating that anions have actually no effect on the fluorescence of NCDs (Fig. S10b†). Above observation confirms that Hg2+ is solely responsible for the quenching.

Although the clear mechanism for good selectivity against other metal ions is still unknown at the present stage, the possible reasons are discussed. The outstanding selectivity and specificity may be attributed to a stronger thermodynamic affinity and faster coordination process of Hg2+ towards the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of NCDs than other metal ions [1,38,47,48]. Furthermore, the coordination interaction of Hg2+ and NCDs facilitates non-radiative recombination of excited electrons in the conduction band with the holes in the valence band through an effective electron transfer process, finally leading to distinguishable fluorescence quenching effects [2,17,48]. Meanwhile, the stability constant between Hg2+ and carboxyl group is higher than those for other metal ions [49], so Hg2+ can interact more strongly with NCDs than other metal ions. Therefore, NCDs show outstanding selectivity towards Hg2+ over other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensitivity of NCDs detection of Hg2+ in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent specificity and high sensitivity of NCDs to Hg2+ imply that the present chemosensor might be directly applied for detecting Hg2+ in real samples. To evaluate the NCDs-based Hg2+ ion sensor in an artificial system, the performance of the present fluorescence sensor for real water sample analysis was also investigated. The tap water samples obtained from our laboratoty without any pretreatment were spiked with standard solutions with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–60μM) and then analyzed with the proposed method. From Fig. S11a†, it is seen that the PL intensity at 440nm (excited at 360nm) gradually decreases with increase in the concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 60μM, which is similar to that measured in deionized water.

The calibration curve for determining Hg2+ in the tap water is obtained by plotting the values of F 0/F versus the concentrations of Hg2+ within the range of 0–60μM (Fig. S11b†). The plot shown in Fig. S11b† does not fit a linear calibration plots over the whole Hg2+ concentration range of 0–60μM, but a good linear correlation is obtained between F 0/F and Hg2+ ion concentration in the range of 0–10μM, which is similar to that for deionized water (F 0/F=0.45369 [Hg2+] +1, R 2=0.99989) (Fig. S11b† inset). LOD for Hg2+ in tap water is calculated to be 1.65nM, slightly higher than 1.48nM for deionized water. Despite the possible interference from minerals and inorganic impurities in the tap water, this sensing system can still sensitively response to 1.65nM Hg2+, which satisfies the sensitivity requirement for Hg2+ detection in drinking water. Above results confirm the validity of this NCDs-based PL sensing method for the detection of Hg2+ in real environmental samples and for the quantification of Hg2+ in practical environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: L-Cys is a typical thiol-containing amino acid and plays a considerable role in biological processes. It can interact with Hg2+ owing to the existence of a thiol group. Therefore, the NCDs–Hg2+ system is considered as a probe for the detection of L-Cys. To research the feasibility of NCDs–Hg2+ fluorescence probe detection of L-Cys, 75μM L-Cys is added into NCDs–Hg2+ system. As shown in Fig. 5, the features of the UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra are restored completely. Owing to strong binding preference between Hg2+ and the thiol groups, Hg2+ is dissociated from the surface of NCDs through the formation of Hg2+–S bond (in other words, the NCDs–Hg2+ complex is dissociated to form a more stable Hg2+-L-Cys complex), which leads to the complete recovery of the UV–vis absorption and emission of NCDs [5,7]. Above results indicate that the NCDs–Hg2+ system can be used as probes for the fluorescence turn-on detection of L-Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the sensitivity of NCDs–Hg2+ system to L-Cys, different concentrations of L-Cys in the range of 0–75μM were added into NCDs–Hg2+ system containing 16μgmL−1 NCDs and 50μM Hg2+. For the original NCDs–Hg2+ solution, there is only a very weak emission at 440nm owing to the quenching effect from Hg2+ ( Fig. 9a). The PL intensity at 440nm of NCDs–Hg2+ system is gradually strengthened with the increasing concentration of L-Cys from 0 to 55μM and then flattens (Fig. 9a), revealing that the NCDs–Hg2+ system is sensitive to L-Cys concentration. Moreover, no spectral shift of the emission band is observed. The fluorescence is completely restored in the presence of 55μM L-Cys, so we chose 55μM L-Cys for the subsequent studies. The relationship between the PL intensity at 440nm of NCDs–Hg2+ system and the concentration of L-Cys is shown in inset of Fig. 9a. The PL enhancement can also be analyzed by the Stern–Volmer equation: F 2/F 1=1+K sv1[Q1] [6], where F 1 and F 2 are the PL intensities at 440nm of NCDs–Hg2+ system in the absence and presence of L-Cys, respectively, K sv1 is the Stern–Volmer constant and [Q 1] is the concentration of L-Cys. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in inset of Fig. 9a does not fit a linear calibration plots over the whole L-Cys concentration range of 0–75μM. In contrast, Fig. 9b shows that a linear plot for the quantitative analysis of L-Cys can be fitted between the F 2/F 1 and the concentration of L-Cys over a range of 0–50μM. The slope of the calibration curve (K sv1) is 0.95065 [μM]−1 and the correlation coefficients (R 2) is 0.99993. The error bars represent variations among three separate measurements. The relative standard deviation is 0.025%, and the LOD of L-Cys in the present system is calculated to be 0.79nM according to Eq. (4). Apparently, the obtained LOD of 0.79nM shows our NCDs–Hg2+ system is highly sensitive to L-Cys, the highly effective quenching capacity of Hg2+ considerably improves the sensitivity of L-Cys. Table 1 shows the comparison of LOD and linear range with those of different CDs–Hg2+ probes for L-Cys detection in the literature [4,5,7,8,50]. Our LOD is remarkable and far below the reported values and the linear range is widest. It is suggested that such NCDs–Hg2+ system as a fluorescent turn-on probe exhibits superior sensitivity for L-Cys, wide linear response range and high possibility for the quantitative detection of L-Cys.

To study the response rate of the fluorescence signal of NCDs-Hg2+ system to L-Cys, the time-dependent PL spectra of the NCDs–Hg2+ system against 55μM Cys were collected at room temperature (Fig. S12†). The data at 0min in Fig. S12† is corresponding to the PL intensity at 440nm of NCDs–Hg2+ system. Obviously, after addition of 55μM L-Cys, the PL intensity at 440nm of NCDs–Hg2+ aqueous solution dramatically increases within 5s and then reaches a plateau, suggesting the restoration rate is very fast and 5s is enough to reach reaction equilibrium between the NCDs–Hg2+ system and L-Cys. Therefore, in the present study, each emission spectrum was collected 5 s after mixing the NCDs–Hg2+ solution and L-Cys.

All the results indicate that NCDs–Hg2+ system can be applied as a fluorescence turn-on probe for the quantitative detection of L-Cys in aqueous solutions with a reasonably high sensitivity and a rapid response. We envision that our assay method could facilitate the biothiol quantification related biological and biomedical research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivityof NCDs detection of L-Cys in deionized water sample
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, to further evaluate the selectivity of NCDs–Hg2+ system to L-Cys, by reading related references [5,7,9,10,14,50], we know that only thiol-containing compounds (Cys, Hcy and GSH) may result in a significant PL enhancement. In contrast, other hydrophilic thiol-free amino acids do not induce any obvious changes of the system's PL properties. In other words, the influence of other thiol-free amino acids can be neglected. So the effects of other thiol-containing species, such as L-GSH and DL-Hcy, on the NCDs–Hg2+ system were investigated. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 10 that the addition of L-GSH, DL-Hcy and L-Cys all results in the fluorescence enhancement to some extent on the NCDs–Hg2+ system. The restoration ability follows the order of L-Cys>L-GSH>DL-Hcy. Most importantly, for L-GSH and DL-Hcy, the fluorescence signal of the system is far lower than that for L-Cys. The results indicate that the developed NCDs–Hg2+ system exhibits excellent selectivity to L-Cys.

In summary, a simple, low-cost and one-pot hydrothermal method was adopted to prepare NCDs from citric acid and ammonia solution. The resultant NCDs display excellent fluorescence properties with a QY as high as 35.4%. The NCDs have been employed for “off–on” detection of Hg2+ and L-Cys with high sensitivity and selectivity. Hg2+ completely quenches the fluorescence of NCDs, which belongs to static quenching, through forming a non-fluorescent stable NCDs–Hg2+ complex (turn off), L-Cys completely resumes the fuorescence of NCDs–Hg2+ system through dissociating NCDs–Hg2+ complex and forming a more stable Hg2+-L-Cys complex with Hg2+–S bond (turn on). The selectivity experiments reveal that the NCDs are specific for Hg2+ or L-Cys even with other metal ion or thiol-containing compound interference. LOD of 1.48nM in the linear detection range of 0–10μM for Hg2+ and LOD of 0.79nM in the linear range of 0–50μM for L-Cys are achieved. Further, the NCDs are applicable for the determination of Hg2+ in tap water samples in the linear ranges of 0–10μM with LOD of 1.65nM. For the easy establishment of stable and strong fluorescence of the NCDs, we believe that the fluorescent probes based on NCDs would be used for the determination of Hg2+ and L-Cys in biological and environmental samples in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21176169), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2012-038), International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (2012DFR50460), and Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (2015013002-10) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.talanta.2016.02.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of polyaniline/carbon dot nanocomposites and their application as a fluorescent probe to detect mercury
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution
Recent progress in luminescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of thiols
A rapid and label-free dual detection of Hg (II) and cysteine with the use of fluorescence switching of graphene quantum dots
A recyclable carbon nanoparticle-based fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercapto biomolecules
Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for "off-on" detection of Cu2+ and L-cysteine in aqueous solution
Sensitive detection of biothiols and histidine based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots-Hg(II) system
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive turn-on detection of glutathione and its cellular imaging
Fluorescent probes for "off-on" sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and L-cysteine based on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and L-cysteine in aqueous solution
Carbon dots functionalized by organosilane with double-sided anchoring for nanomolar Hg2+ detection
Carbon dots-silver nanoparticles fluorescence resonance energy transfer system as a novel turn-on fluorescent probe for selective determination of cysteine
Switch-on fluorescence sensing of glutathione in food samples based on a graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot (g-CNQD)-Hg2+ chemosensor
Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury(II) ions
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Amine-capped carbon dots as a nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of picric acid in aqueous solution via electrostatic interaction
Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
